Part of the problem is that the hype surrounding Nike and Jordan products has fizzled out. The company raised prices, flooded the market with too many releases, oversaturated retro models, and failed to deliver major new innovations. This combination killed much of the resale market by overproducing product and eliminating scarcity. At the same time, the cost of living has skyrocketed, and the core consumer base between the ages of 15 and 40 has been hit hardest by these changes. A significant portion of that group is also aging out of the brand, which makes Nike’s drop in popularity easier to understand.

in order to understand why the stock's value got up to $167 a share in the 4th quarter of 2021 you have to understand what the climate was like for retailers/resellers that were making big money & big profit margins on diff hype shoes & some non-hype shoes that were getting major sells attention during those first 2yrs of the pandemic. Nike was not producing as much stock during those 1st 24 months of the pandemic & so a lot of sought after shoes were flying off the shelves making that certain shoe go up in value in resell price. Buying sneakers during that time period was hard to do as far as walking into your local shoe store because the local shoe stores did not have much to look at during that time period, it was mainly all online where the stock was being bought & the second biggest factor was people spending crazy amounts of money from their pandemic checks. Once that money dried up from the pandemic relief checks that's when the market started to go down & it also did not help Nike when they produced so many pairs of any given shoe to sit & go on discount. I've been rocking New Balance, Adidas & Asics for the quality, price-point & comfortability. Nike is going to have to lower the price tag if they want me buying again any time soon.
